flashcards.back: A systematic process of organizing data into tables to minimize redundancy, avoid anomalies (insertion, deletion, update), and ensure data integrity based on functional dependencies.
A relation is in 1NF if it contains only atomic (indivisible) values. No repeating groups or arrays are allowed. Each column contains only single-valued attributes.
A relation is in 2NF if it is in 1NF and every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on the entire primary key (no partial dependency).
A relation is in 3NF if it is in 2NF and no non-prime attribute transitively depends on the primary key (no transitive dependency: X→Y→Z where X is not a superkey).
A relation is in BCNF if for every non-trivial functional dependency X→Y, X must be a superkey of the relation. Stricter than 3NF.
A constraint between two sets of attributes where specifying values for attribute set X uniquely determines values for attribute set Y, denoted as X→Y.
An attribute that is part of any candidate key of a relation. All attributes in any candidate key are prime attributes.
A functional dependency X→Y where X is not a proper subset of any candidate key, and Y is not a prime attribute. It creates indirect dependency through a third attribute.
A dependency where a non-prime attribute depends on only part of a composite primary key (when primary key has multiple attributes).
Failure to insert data due to absence of other data. Example: Cannot add a new employee without assigning them to a department.
Unintended loss of data when deleting other data. Example: Deleting all students from a course deletes the course information.
Data inconsistency caused by updating one instance but not all duplicates. Example: Changing department name requires updating all employees in that department.
A decomposition where natural join of decomposed relations yields the original relation without spurious tuples. R1 ⋈ R2 = R.
A property where all functional dependencies in the original relation can be enforced using functional dependencies in the decomposed relations without needing joins.
A minimal superkey - a set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples and no proper subset can uniquely identify them. There can be multiple candidate keys.
